questions.specification: Case Study: $\text{MultitoolSoftware}$ has created a $\text{SystemUtilities}$ class that contains methods for string manipulation ($\text{encrypt}$, $\text{format}$), mathematical calculations ($\text{statistics}$, $\text{geometry}$), and file operations ($\text{backup}$, $\text{compress}$), all mixed together in one large class.

parts.content: State what is meant by "class cohesion" in object-oriented design.
parts.markscheme:
- Measures the degree of internal relatedness / how well elements within a class belong together <marks>A1</marks>
- Indicates the extent to which a class is focused on a single purpose or responsibility <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Define internal relatedness
steps.content:
In object-oriented design, **class cohesion** is a measure of how closely the different parts of a class—such as its methods and attributes—relate to one another. 

Ideally, every component inside a class should logically "fit" with the others. If a class has high cohesion, it means all its members are working together to support the same logic.

highlights.text: Measures the degree of internal relatedness / how well elements within a class belong together

steps.title: Focus on single purpose
steps.content:
A key aspect of cohesion is the **Single Responsibility Principle**. This identifies the extent to which a class is dedicated to a single task or functional purpose. 

When a class is focused on one specific job, it is easier to maintain, reuse, and debug. If a class tries to do too many different things, its cohesion decreases.

highlights.text: Indicates the extent to which a class is focused on a single purpose or responsibility

steps.title: Analyze the case study
steps.content:
In the provided example, the `SystemUtilities` class has **low cohesion**. 

This is because it mixes three unrelated responsibilities: 
1. String manipulation 
2. Mathematical calculations 
3. File operations 

Because these elements do not share a single purpose, they do not "belong together" in a well-designed object-oriented system. You can learn more about class design in **Topic B3: Object-oriented programming**.

highlights.text: string manipulation (encrypt, format), mathematical calculations (statistics, geometry), and file operations (backup, compress), all mixed together in one large class.

parts.content: Describe the characteristics of high cohesion and low cohesion classes, explaining why high cohesion is generally preferred.
parts.markscheme:
**High cohesion:**
- A class has a single, well-defined responsibility with closely related methods and data <marks>A1</marks>
- This is preferred because it makes the class easier to understand, maintain, and reuse <marks>A1</marks>

**Low cohesion:**
- A class has multiple unrelated responsibilities or tasks mixed together <marks>A1</marks>
- This makes the class harder to modify and test, as it violates the single responsibility principle <marks>A1</marks>

[4 marks]

steps.title: Define High Cohesion
steps.content: In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), **high cohesion** refers to how focused a class is on a single task. A class with high cohesion has a **single, well-defined responsibility** where all the methods and data are closely related to each other.

highlights.text: A class has a single, well-defined responsibility with closely related methods and data

steps.title: Benefits of High Cohesion
steps.content: High cohesion is generally preferred because it makes the software much more manageable. When a class does one thing well, it is easier for developers to **understand, maintain, and reuse**. For example, if you wanted to use math functions in a different project, a highly cohesive Math class could be moved easily without bringing along unrelated code.

highlights.text: This is preferred because it makes the class easier to understand, maintain, and reuse

steps.title: Define Low Cohesion
steps.content: On the other hand, **low cohesion** occurs when a class has **multiple unrelated responsibilities** or tasks mixed together. In our case study, the $\text{SystemUtilities}$ class shows low cohesion because it handles string manipulation, math, and file operations all in one place.

highlights.text: SystemUtilities
A class has multiple unrelated responsibilities or tasks mixed together

steps.title: Drawbacks of Low Cohesion
steps.content: Low cohesion is discouraged because it **violates the single responsibility principle**. This makes the class **harder to modify and test**; if you change the code for file compression, you might accidentally break a math function, leading to complex bugs and difficult maintenance.

highlights.text: This makes the class harder to modify and test, as it violates the single responsibility principle

steps.title: Define cohesion in OOP
steps.content:
In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), **cohesion** refers to how closely related the functions within a single class are. This concept is vital for designing robust software, as it directly impacts how easy a system is to manage. This topic is part of **Topic B3.1.2: Designing Classes, Their Methods, and Behaviour**.

To answer this question, we will look at the provided $\text{SystemUtilities}$ example to see how mixing different tasks affects the quality of the code.

steps.title: Analyze low cohesion
steps.content:
A **low cohesion** class is one that has multiple unrelated responsibilities or tasks mixed together. 

Looking at our case study, the $\text{SystemUtilities}$ class is a perfect example of low cohesion because it attempts to handle:
- **String manipulation** (e.g., `encrypt`)
- **Mathematical calculations** (e.g., `statistics`)
- **File operations** (e.g., `backup`)

Because these tasks (security, math, and storage) have very little to do with each other, the class becomes "bloated" and violates the **Single Responsibility Principle**.

highlights.text: string manipulation (encrypt, format), mathematical calculations (statistics, geometry), and file operations (backup, compress)

steps.title: Illustrate high cohesion
steps.content:
A **high cohesion** class has a single, well-defined responsibility where all methods and data are closely related. 

To turn our case study into a high-cohesion system, we would split $\text{SystemUtilities}$ into three distinct, specialized classes:
1.  $\text{SecurityManager}$: Handles `encrypt` and `format`.
2.  $\text{MathEngine}$: Handles `statistics` and `geometry`.
3.  $\text{FileManager}$: Handles `backup` and `compress`.

Each of these new classes is highly cohesive because every method inside it serves the same purpose.

steps.title: Justify the preference
steps.content:
High cohesion is generally preferred for several reasons that make life easier for developers:

*   **Maintainability:** If you need to fix a bug in the `statistics` logic, you only look at the $\text{MathEngine}$ class. You don't risk accidentally breaking the `backup` code.
*   **Readability:** It is much easier for a new programmer to understand what a class does if it only has one job.
*   **Reusability:** If a different software project needs to perform string encryption but doesn't need file backups, it can simply reuse the $\text{SecurityManager}$ class without dragging along unnecessary code.

steps.title: Final summary for marks
steps.content:
To ensure you get all 4 marks, your description should include:

1.  **High Cohesion Definition:** A class with a single, well-defined responsibility and related methods ($A1$).
2.  **High Cohesion Benefit:** It makes the class easier to understand, maintain, and reuse ($A1$).
3.  **Low Cohesion Definition:** A class with multiple unrelated responsibilities mixed together ($A1$).
4.  **Low Cohesion Drawback:** It is harder to modify and test because it violates the single responsibility principle ($A1$).

steps.title: Defining High Cohesion via SRP
steps.content:
In Object-Oriented Programming, **high cohesion** occurs when a class strictly follows the **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)**. This means the class has one, and only one, well-defined purpose. All methods and data within that class are closely related to that specific task.

For example, instead of a "do-everything" class, you would have a dedicated `FileManager` class that only handles file operations like backup and compression.

steps.title: Why High Cohesion is Preferred
steps.content:
High cohesion is preferred because it makes code much easier to **maintain and reuse**. 

When a class has a single responsibility, you can modify its logic without worrying about accidentally breaking unrelated features. Furthermore, if you need string manipulation in a different project, it is much easier to export a specific `StringFormatter` class than a massive utility class full of math and file logic you don't need.

steps.title: Identifying Low Cohesion
steps.content:
**Low cohesion** is a direct violation of the Single Responsibility Principle. This happens when a class, like the one in our case study, takes on multiple unrelated responsibilities.

In the `SystemUtilities` example, the class is trying to be a string manipulator, a calculator, and a file manager all at once. This "Swiss Army Knife" approach makes the class bloated and confusing.

highlights.text: SystemUtilities class that contains methods for string manipulation (encrypt, format), mathematical calculations (statistics, geometry), and file operations (backup, compress)

steps.title: Impact on Testing and Debugging
steps.content:
Low cohesion makes **testing and debugging** significantly harder:

*   **Testing**: To test a simple math function, you might have to instantiate a complex object that requires file system access or encryption keys, making unit tests difficult to isolate.
*   **Debugging**: When a bug occurs, it is harder to track down because the state of the object is being manipulated by many unrelated methods. A change in the "format" logic could potentially interfere with "backup" data if they share variables, leading to side effects that are difficult to trace.

parts.content: Using the $\text{MultitoolSoftware}$ case study, analyse the cohesion level of the $\text{SystemUtilities}$ class and suggest improvements.
parts.markscheme:
- The $\text{SystemUtilities}$ class exhibits low cohesion because it combines three unrelated functional areas (string manipulation, mathematical calculations, and file operations) <marks>A1</marks>
- This results in a large, complex class that is difficult to maintain and has multiple reasons to change <marks>A1</marks>
- Cohesion should be improved by refactoring the class into three specialized classes: $\text{StringUtils}$, $\text{MathUtils}$, and $\text{FileUtils}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[3 marks]

steps.title: Categorize functional domains
steps.content:
To analyze the class using **Functional Domain Analysis**, we first group the existing methods into their specific functional areas:

*   **String Domain**: `encrypt`, `format` 
*   **Math Domain**: `statistics`, `geometry` 
*   **File Domain**: `backup`, `compress` 

In Object-Oriented Programming (covered in **Topic B3.1**), a class should ideally focus on a single functional domain. Here, we see three distinct, unrelated areas of responsibility merged into one.

highlights.text: string manipulation
mathematical calculations
file operations

steps.title: Analyze cohesion level
steps.content:
Because the `SystemUtilities` class combines these three unrelated functional areas, it exhibits **low cohesion**. 

In the IB curriculum, **cohesion** refers to how closely related the functions within a single class are. High cohesion is desirable because it means a class does one thing well. Low cohesion makes the class harder to understand and maintain because it lacks a clear, single purpose.

highlights.text: low cohesion because it combines three unrelated functional areas

steps.title: Evaluate maintenance impact
steps.content:
This lack of logical unity creates a large, complex class. From a design perspective, this violates the principle that a class should have only one reason to change. 

For example, if the `encrypt` logic needs an update, you are modifying the same file that handles `backup` operations. This increases the risk of side effects and makes the code base fragile and difficult to manage as it grows.

highlights.text: difficult to maintain and has multiple reasons to change

steps.title: Suggest refactoring improvements
steps.content:
To improve the design, we should refactor `SystemUtilities` into three specialized utility classes, each corresponding to the functional domains identified in Step 0:

1.  `StringUtils`: For `encrypt` and `format` methods.
2.  `MathUtils`: For `statistics` and `geometry` methods.
3.  `FileUtils`: For `backup` and `compress` methods.

This refactoring achieves **high cohesion**, making the software more modular and easier to debug.

highlights.text: refactoring the class into three specialized classes: StringUtils, MathUtils, and FileUtils

steps.title: Assess the cohesion level
steps.content:
In Object-Oriented Programming, **cohesion** refers to how closely related the functions within a single class are. The `SystemUtilities` class exhibits **low cohesion** because it attempts to manage three completely unrelated functional areas: 

1. **String manipulation** (e.g., `encrypt`, `format`)
2. **Mathematical calculations** (e.g., `statistics`, `geometry`)
3. **File operations** (e.g., `backup`, `compress`)

Because these responsibilities share no common logic, the class lacks a single, focused purpose.

highlights.text: string manipulation (encrypt, format)
mathematical calculations (statistics, geometry)
file operations (backup, compress)

steps.title: Analyze using SRP
steps.content:
Applying the **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)**—which states that a class should have only one reason to change—we can see that this class is poorly designed. 

If the encryption algorithm changes, the class must be modified. If the file compression logic changes, the class must be modified. This "mixing" leads to a large, complex class that is difficult to test and maintain, as changes in one area (like math) could inadvertently introduce bugs in unrelated areas (like file handling).

steps.title: Propose a refactored solution
steps.content:
To improve cohesion and follow SRP, we should **refactor** the large `SystemUtilities` class into three specialized classes, each with its own single responsibility:

*   `StringUtils`: For all text-based methods.
*   `MathUtils`: For all calculation-based methods.
*   `FileUtils`: For all storage and file-processing methods.

This makes the code more modular, easier to debug, and simpler to reuse in other parts of the software.

questions.specification: Case Study: SecureAuth Solutions is developing a `UserCredentials` class for their authentication system where password data must be protected from unauthorized access while still allowing password verification for login attempts.

questions.specification: Case Study: DigitalLibrary is creating an e-book management system where Book objects must be properly initialized with title, author, and ISBN as required information, while publication year and publisher are optional details that can be added later.

parts.content: Define what is meant by "object initialization" in object-oriented programming.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that object initialization is the process of setting up an object with appropriate starting values or a defined state when it is first created. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Identify** that this process typically involves memory allocation and the execution of the class constructor code. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define object initialization
steps.content:
In object-oriented programming, **object initialization** is the process of setting an object's initial state by assigning starting values to its attributes when the object is first created (instantiated). 

In the context of the DigitalLibrary, this would be the moment a new `Book` object is created and assigned its mandatory `title`, `author`, and `ISBN`.

highlights.text: properly initialized with title, author, and ISBN as required information

steps.title: Explain technical execution
steps.content:
Technically, initialization involves two key actions performed by the system:
1. **Memory Allocation**: The system reserves a block of memory to store the object's data.
2. **Constructor Execution**: The system runs a special block of code called a **constructor**, which populates that memory with the provided starting values (like the book's title) to ensure the object is in a valid state for use.

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, your definition must include both the purpose (starting state) and the mechanism (constructor/memory).

**Definition:** Object initialization is the process of assigning a defined starting state or values to an object upon creation. This is achieved through memory allocation and the execution of the class constructor.

parts.content: Explain the sequence of events that occur when an object is created, from memory allocation to constructor execution.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that memory for the object is allocated on the heap based on the requirements of the class definition. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Identify** that instance variables are initialized with default values (such as $\texttt{null}$ for objects or $\texttt{0}$ for numeric types). <marks>A1</marks>

- **Explain** that the specific constructor is called to set initial values provided by the user or programmer. <marks>A1</marks>

- **State** that the process concludes by returning an object reference (memory address) to the calling code to allow access to the object. <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Allocate memory on heap
steps.content: The process begins when the keyword $\texttt{new}$ is used. The computer looks at the class definition (like our $\texttt{Book}$ class) to determine how much space is needed. It then allocates a specific block of memory on the **heap** to store the object's data.

highlights.text: memory for the object is allocated on the heap

steps.title: Initialize default values
steps.content:
Once the memory is reserved, but before your specific instructions run, the system performs a "default initialization." All instance variables are set to their standard values based on their data type:

- **Object references** (like $\texttt{String title}$) are set to $\texttt{null}$.
- **Numeric types** (like $\texttt{int publicationYear}$) are set to $0$.
- **Booleans** are set to $\texttt{false}$.

highlights.text: instance variables are initialized with default values

steps.title: Execute the constructor
steps.content: Next, the specific **constructor** for the class is called. This is where the values provided by the programmer (such as the specific $\texttt{title}$, $\texttt{author}$, and $\texttt{ISBN}$) are assigned to the object's variables, replacing the default values from the previous step.

highlights.text: specific constructor is called to set initial values

steps.title: Return object reference
steps.content: Finally, the memory allocation process is completed. The system returns the **memory address** (also known as an **object reference**) to the calling code. This reference is usually stored in a variable, allowing the program to interact with the newly created $\texttt{Book}$ object on the heap.

highlights.text: returning an object reference (memory address)

steps.title: Allocate Memory on Heap
steps.content: When the DigitalLibrary system instantiates a new object (e.g., using the `new` keyword), the computer first allocates a block of memory on the **heap**. The size of this block is determined by the `Book` class definition, ensuring there is enough space to store the title, author, ISBN, publication year, and publisher.

highlights.text: Book

steps.title: Initialize with Default Values
steps.content: Before any specific data is entered, the system initializes the instance variables with **default values**. Since `title`, `author`, and `publisher` are typically objects (Strings), they are initially set to $\texttt{null}$. If the `publicationYear` is stored as a numeric type (like an integer), it is set to $\texttt{0}$.

highlights.text: title, author, and ISBN

steps.title: Execute the Specific Constructor
steps.content: Next, the specific **constructor** for the `Book` class is executed. This is where the values provided by the DigitalLibrary (the actual title, author, and ISBN) are assigned to the object's variables, replacing the default $\texttt{null}$ values with the real data required for the e-book.

highlights.text: properly initialized with title, author, and ISBN

steps.title: Return the Object Reference
steps.content: Finally, the process concludes by returning an **object reference** (the memory address of the heap location). This reference is stored in a variable, allowing the DigitalLibrary system to access and modify the `Book` object's details, such as adding the optional publisher information later.

highlights.text: publication year and publisher are optional details that can be added later

parts.content: Using the DigitalLibrary case study, describe how Book object initialization should be designed to handle both required and optional information.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** a primary constructor $\texttt{Book(String title, String author, String ISBN)}$ that accepts only the required bibliographic parameters. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Design** an overloaded constructor $\texttt{Book(String title, String author, String ISBN, int year, String publisher)}$ to handle instances where optional details are available at creation. <marks>M1</marks>

- **Explain** that the primary constructor should set optional fields to default values (e.g., $\texttt{year = 0}$, $\texttt{publisher = "Unknown"}$) or use constructor chaining ($\texttt{this()}$) to ensure all fields are consistently initialized. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify requirements
steps.content: To design an efficient system for the DigitalLibrary, we need to distinguish between mandatory data and additional details. The question specifies that the **title**, **author**, and **ISBN** are necessary for every book, while the **publication year** and **publisher** might not always be available immediately.

highlights.text: title, author, and ISBN as required information
publication year and publisher are optional details

steps.title: Define the primary constructor
steps.content:
First, we create a **primary constructor**. This constructor only takes the three required parameters. This allows the system to instantiate a `Book` even if we don't have the optional details yet.

$$\texttt{public Book(String title, String author, String ISBN)}$$

This earns the first mark by ensuring the object is valid with only the essential bibliographic data.

steps.title: Design the overloaded constructor
steps.content:
Next, we apply **constructor overloading** by creating a second constructor with a different signature (parameter list). This one accepts both the required and optional parameters:

$$\texttt{public Book(String title, String author, String ISBN, int year, String publisher)}$$

This provides flexibility, allowing the system to capture all details in one step if they are available at the time of creation.

steps.title: Handle optional information
steps.content:
To ensure the object is always in a consistent state, the primary constructor must handle the missing optional fields. There are two common ways to do this:

1.  **Assigning Default Values**: Manually setting $\texttt{year = 0}$ and $\texttt{publisher = "Unknown"}$ inside the primary constructor.
2.  **Constructor Chaining**: Using the $\texttt{this()}$ keyword to call the overloaded constructor from the primary one, passing default values for the missing parts.

Example of chaining:
$$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{public Book(String t, String a, String i) \{} \\ &\quad \texttt{this(t, a, i, 0, "Unknown");} \\ &\texttt{\}} \end{aligned}$$

This approach keeps the code clean and ensures all fields are initialized correctly.

steps.title: Define the overloaded constructor
steps.content:
First, we design an **overloaded constructor** that accepts both the required and optional parameters. This constructor acts as the single point of initialization for all fields in the `Book` class.

$$ \texttt{public Book(String title, String author, String ISBN, int year, String publisher)} $$

Inside this constructor, we assign all five parameters to the class's instance variables. This ensures that when all data is available, the object is fully populated immediately.

highlights.text: publication year and publisher are optional details

steps.title: Implement the primary constructor
steps.content:
Next, we define the **primary constructor** which only requires the mandatory bibliographic information: `title`, `author`, and `ISBN`.

$$ \texttt{public Book(String title, String author, String ISBN)} $$

This constructor is used when the optional details (year and publisher) are not yet known at the time of the object's creation.

highlights.text: title, author, and ISBN as required information

steps.title: Apply constructor chaining
steps.content:
To ensure consistent initialization and avoid code duplication, we use **constructor chaining** via the `this()` keyword. 

The primary constructor calls the overloaded constructor by passing the required fields along with **default values** for the optional ones (e.g., `0` for the year and `"Unknown"` for the publisher):

$$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{public Book(String title, String author, String ISBN) \{} \\ &\quad \texttt{this(title, author, ISBN, 0, "Unknown");} \\ &\texttt{\}} \end{aligned}$$

This design fulfills the IB requirement to handle both sets of information while maintaining a single path for variable assignment.

steps.title: Identify required vs. optional fields
steps.content:
In Object-Oriented Programming (Topic B3.1), we distinguish between state that is essential for an object to exist and state that is extra detail. 

Based on the specification, the **required** fields are:
- `title` (String)
- `author` (String)
- `ISBN` (String)

The **optional** fields are:
- `year` (int)
- `publisher` (String)

highlights.text: title, author, and ISBN as required information
publication year and publisher are optional details

steps.title: Define the minimal constructor
steps.content:
The **Minimal Constructor** approach starts by defining a primary constructor that only accepts the mandatory parameters. This ensures every `Book` object is valid as soon as it is created.

$$\texttt{public Book(String title, String author, String ISBN)}$$

Inside this constructor, you should also initialize the optional fields to **default values** (like $0$ or `"Unknown"`) to maintain consistency.

highlights.text: Define a primary constructor Book(String title, String author, String ISBN) that accepts only the required bibliographic parameters.

steps.title: Provide mutator (setter) methods
steps.content:
To handle the optional information that might be "added later," we provide **setter methods** (mutators). This allows the object to remain flexible.

$$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{public void setYear(int year)} \\ &\texttt{public void setPublisher(String publisher)} \end{aligned}$$

This approach is ideal for the DigitalLibrary system because it doesn't force the user to have all the data ready at the moment of instantiation.

highlights.text: optional details that can be added later

steps.title: Implement constructor chaining
steps.content:
For maximum flexibility, we can design an **overloaded constructor** that accepts all five parameters. To keep the code clean and avoid repetition, we use **constructor chaining** ($ \texttt{this()} $).

This allows a user to either:
1. Create a book with just the basics and use setters later.
2. Create a book with all details immediately if they are available.

highlights.text: Design an overloaded constructor Book(String title, String author, String ISBN, int year, String publisher)
use constructor chaining (this()) to ensure all fields are consistently initialized.

questions.specification: Case Study: TechStart Inc. is developing a customer relationship management (CRM) system. Their current system uses separate variables and functions scattered across multiple files, making it difficult to maintain customer data consistency and add new features.

parts.content: Define what is meant by "object-oriented programming" (OOP).
parts.markscheme:
- Define object-oriented programming as a programming paradigm based on the concept of "objects" which contain both data (attributes) and methods (behaviours) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that these objects interact with each other to solve complex problems and model real-world entities <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the four fundamental principles of object-oriented programming and describe how each contributes to better software design.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain encapsulation as bundling data and methods together and hiding internal implementation, which improves data security and reduces coupling <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain inheritance as creating new classes based on existing ones, which promotes code reuse and logical hierarchical organization <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain polymorphism as allowing objects of different types to respond to the same interface differently, increasing system flexibility and extensibility <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain abstraction as focusing on essential features while hiding complex implementation details, which simplifies the design for the developer <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the TechStart CRM case study, analyse why an object-oriented approach would be more suitable than procedural programming for this type of application.
parts.markscheme:
- Analyse how an object-oriented approach models real-world business entities (e.g., customers, contacts, and sales) naturally in the CRM system, making it more intuitive to manage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Analyse how encapsulation protects customer data integrity and ensures consistent access patterns, solving the issue of scattered variables and functions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Analyse how the modularity of OOP supports better maintainability and easier addition of new features as business requirements evolve <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Case Study: WeatherStation Corp is developing a smart thermostat system that monitors and controls room temperature. The system needs to track current temperature, validate temperature readings, and provide safe access to temperature data for the HVAC system.

parts.content: Define what is meant by "method" in object-oriented programming.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** a method as a function that belongs to a class and operates on objects of that class <marks>R1</marks>

- **State** that a method defines the behaviour or actions that objects can perform <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the structural component
steps.content:
In object-oriented programming (OOP), a **method** is defined structurally as a function or subroutine that is declared within a class. Unlike a standalone function, a method is bound to the class and is designed to operate specifically on the data (attributes) held by the objects instantiated from that class.

This earns the first mark by identifying its relationship to the class structure.

highlights.text: Define what is meant by "method"

steps.title: Define the behavioral role
steps.content:
From a behavioral perspective, a method defines the **actions** or **behaviors** that an object can perform. While the class's variables store the "state" of the object, the methods determine how that object interacts with the rest of the system.

For example, in the **WeatherStation Corp** case study, a `Thermostat` object might have a state (the current temperature) and a behavior (the method used to `validateTemperature()`).

highlights.text: validate temperature readings

steps.title: Consolidate the 2-mark answer
steps.content:
To secure both marks on an IB exam, your definition should combine these two aspects:

1.  **Structural**: A method is a function that belongs to a class and operates on its objects.
2.  **Behavioral**: It defines the actions or behaviors that those objects can perform.

This concept is a core part of **Topic B3.1.2** regarding class design and behavior.

steps.title: Contextualizing the Smart Thermostat
steps.content: To understand what a **method** is, let's look at the **WeatherStation** case study. In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), we would create a `Thermostat` class to represent the physical device. This class doesn't just hold data (like the temperature); it also needs to *do* things. This is where methods come in!

steps.title: Defining the Method
steps.content:
A **method** is a function that belongs to a specific class and operates on the data (objects) of that class. 

In our scenario, a method like `validateReading()` would live inside the `Thermostat` class. It specifically operates on the `currentTemperature` data stored within that specific thermostat object to ensure the data is accurate before it's used.

highlights.text: validate temperature readings

steps.title: Methods as Behaviors
steps.content:
Crucially, a method defines the **behavior** or **actions** that an object can perform. 

While the temperature itself is just a value, the *action* of the thermostat providing "safe access to temperature data for the HVAC system" is a behavior. This would be implemented as a method, such as `getTemperature()`, which defines exactly how the thermostat interacts with the HVAC system.

highlights.text: provide safe access to temperature data for the HVAC system

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks on the exam, your definition should include these two points:

1. A method is a **function belonging to a class** that operates on its objects (e.g., the `validateReading()` function in the `Thermostat` class).
2. It defines the **actions or behaviors** of the object (e.g., the process of checking if a temperature is within a safe range).

You can find more about this in **Topic B3.1: Fundamentals of OOP for a single class**.

steps.title: Data vs. Action
steps.content:
To understand a **method**, it is best to compare it to its partner: the **attribute**. In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), a class is like a blueprint. This blueprint contains two things:
1.  **Attributes**: These are the 'nouns' or data the object stores (e.g., the thermostat's `currentTemperature`).
2.  **Methods**: These are the 'verbs' or actions that the object can perform.

highlights.text: current temperature

steps.title: Defining the Action
steps.content:
A method is specifically defined as the **behaviour** or the **actions** that an object can perform. While attributes define the state of the thermostat, the method defines what it can actually do with that information.

In the WeatherStation Corp case, an action like `validate temperature readings` would be implemented as a method because it is a specific operation the system performs.

highlights.text: validate temperature readings

steps.title: Operating on the Object
steps.content:
Relationality is key: a method is a **function that belongs to a class** and is designed to **operate on objects** of that class. It often uses or modifies the data stored in the object's attributes to complete its task.

For example, a method named `updateTemp()` would interact with the attribute `currentTemperature` to ensure the data is accurate for the HVAC system.

highlights.text: HVAC system

steps.title: Final Definition
steps.content:
To earn full marks on the IB exam, your definition should include these two specific points:

1.  A method is a function that belongs to a class and **operates on objects** of that class.
2.  It defines the **behaviour or actions** that objects can perform.

parts.content: Explain the difference between accessor methods (getters) and mutator methods (setters), providing examples of when each would be used.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that accessor methods (getters) return the value of private attributes without modifying them <marks>R1</marks>

- **State** an example of an accessor method, such as $\text{getCurrentTemperature()}$, used to retrieve a current reading for display <marks>A1</marks>

- **Explain** that mutator methods (setters) modify the value of private attributes, often incorporating validation logic <marks>R1</marks>

- **State** an example of a mutator method, such as $\text{setTargetTemperature(temp)}$, used to update a desired temperature with range checking <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Define Accessor Methods
steps.content:
Accessor methods, commonly known as **getters**, are designed to be "read-only." Their primary role is to retrieve the value of a private attribute from an object without making any changes to that data. This supports the principle of encapsulation by allowing other parts of the system to see the data without being able to corrupt it.

In our WeatherStation system, an example would be a method called `getCurrentTemperature()`. The HVAC system would call this method to read the current room temperature so it can decide whether to turn on the heating or cooling.

highlights.text: monitors and controls room temperature

steps.title: Define Mutator Methods
steps.content: Mutator methods, or **setters**, are "write-capable" methods. They are used to modify or update the value of a private attribute. Unlike accessors, mutators often include **validation logic** to ensure that the new data is sensible and safe before it is saved to the object's memory.

steps.title: Apply Mutators to Scenario
steps.content:
For the smart thermostat, a mutator example would be `setTargetTemperature(newTemp)`. 

Before updating the internal `targetTemperature` variable, this method would perform a range check—for instance, ensuring the user hasn't tried to set the temperature to a dangerous level like $100^{\circ}C$ or $-50^{\circ}C$. This provides the "safe access" mentioned in the case study.

highlights.text: provide safe access to temperature data

steps.title: Review Markscheme Requirements
steps.content:
To secure all 4 marks, ensure your explanation covers these points:

1.  **Accessor (1 mark):** Explain it returns a value without modifying it.
2.  **Accessor Example (1 mark):** Provide a relevant example like `getCurrentTemperature()`.
3.  **Mutator (1 mark):** Explain it modifies a value and often includes validation.
4.  **Mutator Example (1 mark):** Provide a relevant example like `setTargetTemperature(temp)` with range checking.

This concept is a core part of **Topic B3.1.5: Encapsulation and Information Hiding**.

steps.title: Define Accessor Methods
steps.content:
An **accessor method**, commonly known as a "getter," is a method used to retrieve the value of a private attribute from an object. Its primary purpose is to provide read-only access to data without allowing the external program to modify the internal state of the object directly.

In the context of the WeatherStation Corp system, an accessor would be used when the HVAC system needs to check the current temperature to decide whether to turn on the heating or cooling.

highlights.text: accessor methods (getters) return the value of private attributes without modifying them

steps.title: Accessor Pseudocode Example
steps.content:
To illustrate, here is how an accessor method for the `currentTemperature` attribute would look in IB standard pseudocode. Notice that it uses a `return` statement and does not perform any assignments to the attribute.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text{method getCurrentTemperature()} \\ & \quad \text{return currentTemperature} \\ & \text{end method} \end{aligned}$$

This method allows the system to "see" the temperature safely.

highlights.text: getCurrentTemperature()

steps.title: Define Mutator Methods
steps.content:
A **mutator method**, or "setter," is used to modify or update the value of a private attribute. Unlike accessors, mutators often include **validation logic** to ensure that the new data is sensible or safe before the change is applied. This protects the integrity of the object's data (a key part of encapsulation).

For a thermostat, a mutator would be used to change the target temperature requested by a user.

highlights.text: mutator methods (setters) modify the value of private attributes, often incorporating validation logic

steps.title: Mutator Pseudocode Example
steps.content:
Here is a mutator method for `targetTemperature`. It takes a parameter (`newTemp`) and checks if it falls within a safe range (e.g., 15°C to 30°C) before updating the attribute.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text{method setTargetTemperature(newTemp)} \\ & \quad \text{if newTemp >= 15 AND newTemp <= 30 then} \\ & \quad \quad \text{targetTemperature = newTemp} \\ & \quad \text{else} \\ & \quad \quad \text{output "Error: Temperature out of safe range"} \\ & \quad \text{end if} \\ & \text{end method} \end{aligned}$$

By using this method instead of direct access, the system prevents a user from accidentally setting the thermostat to a dangerous level, like 500°C.

highlights.text: setTargetTemperature(temp)

steps.title: Summary of Key Differences
steps.content:
To summarize for your exam:

*   **Accessors:** Purpose is to **read**; they return a value and have no parameters.
*   **Mutators:** Purpose is to **write/update**; they take a parameter and usually include validation to maintain data integrity.

These concepts are fundamental to **Topic B3.1.5: Encapsulation and Information Hiding**.

parts.content: Using the WeatherStation case study, describe what accessor and mutator methods would be appropriate for the $\text{Thermostat}$ class and explain any validation that should be included.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** appropriate accessor methods for the case study, such as $\text{getCurrentTemperature()}$ and $\text{getTargetTemperature()}$ for use by the HVAC system and user interface <marks>A1</marks>

- **State** an appropriate mutator method, such as $\text{setTargetTemperature(temp)}$, to update the desired temperature setting <marks>A1</marks>

- **Explain** that validation should check that the temperature is within a reasonable range (e.g., $10\text{--}35^\circ\text{C}$) to prevent sensor malfunction errors or unsafe settings <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify Internal Attributes
steps.content:
In Object-Oriented Design, we start by identifying the data the class needs to store. For a $\text{Thermostat}$, we need two primary internal attributes: 
1. `currentTemp`: To store the actual room temperature.
2. `targetTemp`: To store the desired temperature set by the user.

Following the principle of **encapsulation**, these attributes should be private to prevent direct, unauthorized access from outside the class.

highlights.text: track current temperature

steps.title: Design Accessor Methods
steps.content:
Accessor methods (also known as **getters**) allow external objects, such as an HVAC controller or a user interface, to read the private data. For this class, we should implement:
- `getCurrentTemperature()`: Returns the value of `currentTemp`.
- `getTargetTemperature()`: Returns the value of `targetTemp`.

These methods provide "read-only" access, which is a core part of encapsulation.

highlights.text: getCurrentTemperature()
getTargetTemperature()

steps.title: Design Mutator Methods
steps.content:
Mutator methods (also known as **setters**) allow external objects to change the internal state. We need a method to update the user's desired setting:
- `setTargetTemperature(temp)`: Updates the `targetTemp` attribute.

We typically do **not** provide a setter for `currentTemp` to the user, as that value should only be updated by the system's hardware sensors.

highlights.text: setTargetTemperature(temp)

steps.title: Implement Range-Check Validation
steps.content:
The main advantage of using a mutator (setter) is that we can include **validation** logic. Before updating `targetTemp`, the method should check if the value is within a safe, realistic range.

$$\text{if } (temp \geq 10 \text{ and } temp \leq 35) \text{ then update data}$$ 

This range-check (e.g., $10\text{--}35^\circ\text{C}$) is crucial because it prevents the HVAC system from attempting to reach unsafe temperatures or reacting to faulty sensor data.

highlights.text: validate temperature readings
within a reasonable range (e.g., 10–35°C)
